---
title: "Project2Approach"
author: "Jonnathan Zuna Largo"
format: html
editor: visual
---

## Introduction

Project 2 explores data tidying and transformation in R using three distinct wide format datasets pulled from real-world sources: CONMEBOL 2026 FIFA World Cup qualification standings, a global list of cities ranked by average monthly precipitation, and historical NBA per game statistics from Basketball Reference. Each dataset shares the same structural problem in that key variables are spread across multiple columns rather than being organized into a clean key-value format, making them difficult to analyze directly. The goal of this assignment is to apply the tidyr and dplyr packages to reshape all three datasets from wide to tidy long format, normalize variable structures, handle missing or inconsistent values, and then perform a meaningful analysis on each cleaned dataset. Together these three sources offer a range of contexts, from sports and climate to international soccer, that will demonstrate how the same core tidying principles can be applied across very different data environments.

## Approach

The workflow for all three datasets follows the same general pipeline, beginning with saving each source as a raw csv file in its original wide format and committing those files to GitHub before any transformation takes place. For the CONMEBOL dataset, I would copy the qualification standings table from Wikipedia into a spreadsheet, preserving columns like wins, draws, losses, goals scored, and points as separate variables for each team. The precipitation dataset would be saved similarly, with each city occupying one row and the twelve months of the year spread across twelve columns. The NBA data would either be exported directly from Basketball Reference as a csv or scraped, with each season as a row and per game statistics like points, assists, rebounds, and three point attempts filling the columns.

Once all three raw files are committed, each dataset I would use read.csv() to import the file, then apply pivot_longer() to collapse the wide columns into a variable and value pair, resulting in tidy long format data where every row represents one observation. I would use rename_with(tolower) across all three to normalize column naming conventions and apply filter(), drop_na(), or mutate() as needed to address missing values, non numeric entries like dashes representing zero, and any mid-table header rows that Basketball Reference sometimes inserts. From there, each dataset would go through its own dplyr-based analysis: the CONMEBOL data would be summarized by team to compare points and goal differentials, the precipitation data would be grouped by city and month to identify seasonal patterns, and the NBA data would be filtered to key metrics and visualized as trend lines over time. All outputs would include clearly labeled ggplot2 visualizations and short written interpretations contextualizing what the numbers show.

## Roadblocks

Across all three datasets, the most recurring challenge is likely to be data quality issues that only become visible after importing into R. The Wikipedia tables for both CONMEBOL and precipitation data may contain special characters, merged header rows, or inconsistently formatted values that require cleaning before pivot_longer() can run cleanly, and the precipitation table in particular may mix millimeter and inch measurements across cities without an obvious indicator column, which would require careful unit conversion using mutate() before any cross-city comparisons are valid. The NBA dataset introduces a different kind of problem in that Basketball Reference uses JavaScript rendering on some of its pages, which can make straightforward scraping unreliable and may require downloading the data manually or using the httr package to retrieve it properly. There is also the shared issue across all three sources that some rows or cells may represent incomplete observations, whether due to an ongoing qualification campaign, a city with missing monthly records, or a shortened NBA season caused by a lockout, and I would need to make deliberate and documented decisions in each case about whether to exclude those rows with drop_na() or retain them with clear flags so the analysis remains transparent and reproducible.
